primary_diagnosis.name: Tinea cruris (dermatophyte infection of the groin) with a topical-corticosteroid-modified acute flare (tinea incognito)

differential.diagnosis: tinea cruris (dermatophyte infection of the groin)
differential.evidence_basis: A chronic (3-week), well-demarcated, intensely itchy scaly plaque in the groin creases with a raised, arcuate, advancing scaly border, central clearing/post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, and — critically — sparing of the scrotum and penis, in a sweaty heavy labourer with a background of recurrent tinea and athlete's foot. This is the classic macroscopic picture of tinea cruris. The recent explosive worsening after applying a potent topical corticosteroid is the hallmark of tinea incognito (steroid-modified tinea), where local immunosuppression lets the dermatophyte flourish.

differential.diagnosis: candidal intertrigo (cutaneous candidiasis of the groin)
differential.evidence_basis: Candida also thrives in warm moist flexures, but it characteristically produces a uniform beefy-red macerated erythema WITH satellite papules/pustules and typically involves the scrotum — none of which fit here. The distinct advancing scaly border, central clearing and scrotal sparing, and the absence of satellite pustules, favour a dermatophyte over Candida.

Flexural psoriasis presents as well-demarcated erythema in body folds, but it is typically a smooth, shiny, moist, NON-scaly plaque without an advancing scaly border or central clearing, and is usually symmetrical rather than showing an arcuate creeping edge. The prominent advancing scale and central clearing here argue for tinea. (Erythrasma — a Corynebacterium overgrowth — is also on the flexural differential but is typically only mildly itchy, flat, and finely wrinkled with a brown coral-red Wood's-lamp fluorescence, unlike this intensely pruritic, raised, scaly, inflammatory border; a confident SNOMED candidate for erythrasma was not available so it is folded here per protocol section 8 rather than coded speculatively.)

differential.diagnosis: secondary bacterial cellulitis of the groin/thigh
differential.evidence_basis: A raw, macerated, weeping flare in a body fold could become secondarily bacterially infected, and the triage call raised this. It must be actively screened for, but here it is screened NEGATIVE on patient-obtainable data: the patient is afebrile and systemically well, there is no rapidly spreading hot induration beyond the rash, no fluctuance and no purulent foul discharge. It remains the key red-flag to safety-net against, not the current diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should frame this as a common groin dermatophyte infection (tinea) whose recent flare is driven by inappropriate potent-steroid use, while actively screening for — and here excluding on patient-reportable data — secondary bacterial cellulitis. It should NOT anchor on candidiasis, and should NOT treat the presentation as an emergency.

differential.diagnosis: tinea cruris with topical-corticosteroid-modified flare (tinea incognito)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic tinea cruris morphology plus a clear temporal link between potent topical corticosteroid application and an explosive inflammatory flare, with a NEGATIVE systemic/cellulitis screen (afebrile, well, no spreading hot induration, no pus). The management pivot is ceasing the steroid and starting a dermatophyte-directed antifungal.

differential.diagnosis: secondary bacterial cellulitis of the groin/thigh
differential.evidence_basis: Screened negative: no fever, no rigors, no rapidly spreading hot tender induration beyond the rash, no fluctuance, no purulent discharge, patient systemically well. Antibiotics are not indicated; this stays as the safety-net trigger rather than an active diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen confirms a steroid-modified dermatophyte flare in a systemically well, afebrile patient with no features of bacterial cellulitis — a T2 GP/telehealth-manageable disposition with prominent safety-netting, not an urgent in-person or emergency presentation.

differential.diagnosis: tinea cruris (tinea incognito flare from potent topical corticosteroid use)
differential.evidence_basis: Clinically classic tinea cruris exacerbated into tinea incognito by 4 days of potent topical betamethasone. Definitive confirmation would be a skin-scraping for microscopy (KOH) and dermatophyte culture, which telehealth cannot perform; the clinical diagnosis is confident enough to direct steroid cessation and antifungal therapy while safety-netting for secondary infection. Candidal intertrigo, inverse psoriasis and erythrasma remain formal alternatives resolved by response to therapy or mycology if the picture is atypical or fails to settle.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A telehealth-appropriate answer names a common groin fungal infection worsened by a strong steroid cream, instructs immediate cessation of that steroid, directs antifungal therapy (topical, plus oral via the prescribing GP given the severity and extent), gives hygiene/barrier and re-infection advice, and safety-nets for secondary bacterial infection — WITHOUT over-triaging a stable, afebrile tinea to urgent in-person or emergency care, and WITHOUT continuing/recommending a topical corticosteroid or a steroid–antifungal combination.

red_flags.description: Inappropriate potent topical corticosteroid use on an undiagnosed groin rash, driving a tinea incognito flare with skin-barrier breakdown and steroid-induced local atrophy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Potent topical corticosteroids suppress local immunity, allowing the dermatophyte to spread and the rash to become atypical, rawer and more inflamed while masking the diagnosis; continued use worsens the infection and thins the skin. The pivotal safe action is to stop the steroid and treat the fungus.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continuing the steroid (or adding a steroid–antifungal combination) perpetuates and spreads the infection, deepens skin atrophy and delays cure.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis of the macerated flexure — screened NEGATIVE at baseline but the key deterioration to safety-net against
red_flags.clinical_significance: A raw, weeping, macerated groin flare can become secondarily bacterially infected; systemic features (fever, rigors), rapidly spreading hot tender induration, fluctuance or purulent foul discharge would signal cellulitis/abscess needing urgent in-person antibiotics or drainage. At presentation the patient is afebrile, systemically well and shows none of these — so it is excluded now but is the explicit escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net could allow an evolving secondary cellulitis or abscess to progress unrecognised in a patient managed remotely for a 'fungal rash'.

pathophysiology_summary: Tinea cruris is a superficial dermatophyte infection of the groin (most often Trichophyton rubrum), which digests keratin in the stratum corneum and provokes an inflammatory response at the advancing margin — producing the characteristic well-demarcated, arcuate, scaly, actively-spreading border with central clearing and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Warmth, sweating, occlusion by tight synthetic clothing and an autoinoculation reservoir of tinea pedis (the 'foot-to-groin' route when dressing) all favour it. Applying a potent topical corticosteroid suppresses the local cell-mediated immune response: the dermatophyte proliferates and spreads while the surface inflammation is blunted and distorted, giving the atypical, rawer, less-scaly, more extensive picture termed tinea incognito, plus steroid-induced epidermal atrophy and telangiectasia. Definitive dermatophyte-directed therapy (topical allylamine/azole antifungal, with oral terbinafine for extensive, refractory or steroid-modified disease) plus cessation of the corticosteroid, drying/hygiene measures and treatment of the tinea pedis reservoir achieves cure. Barrier breakdown in the macerated creases creates a portal for secondary bacterial infection, which is the principal complication to monitor for.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to identify the potent topical corticosteroid as the driver of the flare, and continuing it, adding a new steroid, or prescribing a combined corticosteroid–antifungal cream (e.g. clotrimazole–betamethasone) — all of which keep suppressing local immunity and spreading the dermatophyte.

Mislabelling the flexural rash as candidal intertrigo and giving anticandidal-only therapy (e.g. nystatin), when the scrotal sparing, advancing scaly border and absence of satellite pustules point to a dermatophyte.

Reflexively escalating a systemically-well, afebrile patient with a cellulitis-negative screen to urgent same-day in-person care or the emergency department because the rash looks 'severe' and painful — over-triaging a common GP/telehealth-manageable fungal infection.
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for a raw-looking but non-infected fungal flare (no fever, no spreading hot induration, no pus) — an error of commission that does not treat the fungus.
Treating the groin alone and not addressing the concurrent tinea pedis and the sweat/friction/occlusion factors, leading to prompt re-infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the groin/thigh for deep induration, fluctuance or tenderness to definitively exclude secondary cellulitis or abscess
Skin-scraping for potassium hydroxide (KOH) microscopy and dermatophyte culture to confirm the fungus and exclude mimics
Direct assessment of the degree of steroid-induced epidermal atrophy and the true depth of skin breakdown in the creases
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the classic tinea cruris morphology and the steroid-driven (tinea incognito) flare; instruct IMMEDIATE cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid; recommend a topical dermatophyte-directed antifungal (e.g. terbinafine 1% cream) and, given the severity, extent and steroid modification, arrange oral antifungal therapy (oral terbinafine) via the prescribing GP; give drying/hygiene, loose-cotton-clothing and barrier-cream advice and advise treating the athlete's foot; and safety-net explicitly for secondary bacterial infection. This is a T2 GP/telehealth-manageable disposition — NOT an emergency and NOT a reflex urgent in-person referral.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the morphology and steroid history are classic). The real risks are OVER-triage (escalating a stable, afebrile, cellulitis-negative tinea to urgent in-person/ED) and errors of commission (continuing a topical steroid, adding a steroid–antifungal combination, giving antibiotics, or anticandidal-only therapy). The safety-net for an evolving secondary cellulitis is the genuine deterioration to watch.
